alsoKnownAs: Pseudosphromenus dayi (sister species, visually distinct, similar care)

nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish and still waters including rice paddies, swamps, weedy ponds, and the densely-vegetated margins of slow rivers and ditches; tolerates a wide range of conditions and is sometimes encountered close to tidal influence.
nativeRange.biotope: Lowland rice paddy / weedy pond / slow vegetated stream

decorPreferences: Dense planting including floating plants (subdued lighting preferred)
Driftwood, leaf litter, and shaded cavities (half coconut shells, upturned flowerpots) for spawning sites
Soft, sluggish flow (sponge filter ideal); avoid strong currents
Tight-fitting lid essential (labyrinth fish; needs warm humid air at the surface)

diet.notes: In the wild the Spike-Tailed Paradise Fish feeds primarily on insect larvae and small invertebrates. In captivity it tends to do best on small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, Artemia, white worm, grindal worm, bloodworm, and small insects like Drosophila or pinhead crickets. Dried and prepared foods may be accepted occasionally but are generally not eaten in sufficient quantities on their own to maintain the fish in optimal long-term condition.

goodWith: Small peaceful cyprinids from similar habitats (e.g. small rasboras, danionins)
Small Corydoras and other peaceful bottom-dwellers
avoidWith: Boisterous or fast-moving community fish that outcompete them at feeding
Similarly-shaped or larger anabantoids (Betta, Macropodus, Trichogaster) — risk of harassment
Dwarf shrimp fry (likely predated; adult shrimp are usually safe in well-planted setups)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by renjus box (2048x1511). Sharp full lateral view of a live adult clearly showing the diagnostic elongated spike/lance caudal extension, reddish anal-fin margin, and the species' understated brown/silver coloration; best diagnostic framing of the set.

Photo by renjus box (2048x1284). Live handled specimen, sharp whole-body lateral with silvery-blue flank iridescence and pointed spiketail visible; alternate angle for variety. Out-of-water hand shot.
Photo by renjus box (2048x1364). Two individuals, one displaying a dramatically long caudal spike extension (the species' namesake feature); useful supporting shot. Slightly cluttered composition.

summary: A small, peaceful labyrinth fish from southern India and Sri Lanka, the Spike-Tailed Paradise Fish (Pseudosphromenus cupanus) is prized among anabantoid enthusiasts for its understated coloration, expressive finnage, and approachable breeding behaviour. Best kept as a pair in a heavily planted, gently filtered tank.
careNotes: The Spike-Tailed Paradise Fish (Pseudosphromenus cupanus) is a small osphronemid native to sluggish, vegetated lowland waters of southern India and Sri Lanka. As a labyrinth fish it breathes atmospheric air at the surface, so a tight-fitting lid is important both for escape prevention and to maintain a layer of warm, humid air above the water — this matters especially for developing fry. The species tends to be peaceful but timid; it does best in a quiet, heavily planted tank with subdued lighting (floating plants help diffuse light naturally), gentle flow (a sponge filter is a common choice), and ample cover from driftwood, leaf litter, and cavities such as half coconut shells. Adults reach roughly 6 to 7.5 cm, and at that size boisterous tankmates can easily outcompete them at feeding; a species-only setup or a carefully chosen community of similarly sized, peaceful fish generally yields the best results. Compatible tankmates include small peaceful cyprinids, small loaches, and Corydoras-type catfish; other anabantoids and fin-nippers are generally best avoided. A closely related species, Pseudosphromenus dayi (sometimes called Day's Spiketail or the Brown Spike-tailed Paradise Fish), looks noticeably different — males display two dark lateral stripes and a longer caudal extension — but the two species are thought to have broadly similar care requirements and are often confused with one another in the trade.
breedingNotes: The Spike-Tailed Paradise Fish is a bubble nester, but with an unusual twist: males have been reported to construct nests inside cavities or under overhangs — such as half coconut shells or upturned flowerpots placed in the tank — rather than openly at the surface, which is atypical for osphronemids and reflects the species' generally secretive nature. Males have also been observed periodically transferring eggs between multiple nest sites by carrying them in the mouth, a behaviour that blurs the line between strict bubble-nesting and mouth-brooding. Spawning follows the typical anabantoid embrace, and egg counts have been reported reaching up to several hundred per spawn. Incubation tends to be rapid (around 24 to 48 hours), with fry becoming free-swimming after a further 2 to 3 days. Males have been observed guarding both eggs and early fry; females are typically removed after spawning. The fry are very small and require infusoria-grade food initially, graduating to microworm and baby Artemia nauplii as they grow. Slightly raising the temperature and conditioning the pair with live foods, in soft, slightly acidic water, has been reported to help trigger spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary because designated Aquarium Co-Op product page (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/spike-tailed-paradise-fish) returned HTTP 404. SeriouslyFish provided the most complete husbandry account: water parameters, tank dimensions, breeding behaviour, native range, diet, and compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Osphronemidae / Anabantiformes), max size (~7.5 cm TL), temperature (24–27°C), pH (6.5–7.8), hardness (≤15 dH), and native range. Notes paternal egg-transfer behaviour (mouth-carrying between bubble nest sites).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy, native range (southern India and Sri Lanka), habitat (paddy fields, ditches, slow water), bubble-nest / mouth-transfer breeding behaviour, and synonymy with Macropodus cupanus / Polyacanthus cupanus.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish (Pseudosphromenus dayi)
additional.notes: Sister species reference per hybrid-consolidation policy: confirmed P. dayi shares the same water parameters, tank requirements, and bubble-nest breeding biology as P. cupanus but is visually distinct (two dark lateral stripes, longer caudal extension in males).

fish.breedingNotes: Males have been reported to build bubble nests inside caves or cavities (such as half coconut shells) rather than openly at the surface, and have also been observed carrying eggs between nest sites in the mouth. Pairs spawn via the typical anabantoid embrace. The fry are very small and require infusoria-grade food before graduating to microworm and baby brine shrimp. Removing the female after spawning and rearing the fry separately is generally advised.
